entry.title: MAD-HTLC
entry.summary:
The discussion centers on enhancing the security of the Lightning Network (LN) protocol by introducing mechanisms to deter dishonest behavior, specifically focusing on attacks involving bribery. A suggested approach to discourage such attacks includes the use of bribery proofs for failed attempts. This method involves honest users adhering to protocol guidelines by not prematurely revealing their timelocked transactions. If an attacker like Bob were to attempt bribery and fail, it could be proven to external parties by showcasing a signed timelocked transaction from Bob that attempts to spend outputs previously spent by another transaction before the locktime expired. Such activity would reveal Bob's dishonest intentions as these transactions should not be possible if he had complied with the timelock.
Moreover, these proofs could be distributed within the network, potentially leading to a scenario where network participants might decide against peering with individuals like Bob upon recognizing these proofs. To further safeguard against such attacks, the implementation of a scoring or reputation system is proposed. This system would limit exposure based on historical public channel records and possibly incorporate fidelity bonds. These modifications aim to complicate the process for attackers using new or disposable identities to compromise the network. Additionally, while Bob could opt to send these bribery transactions privately to selected miners, keeping these transactions non-public would likely diminish the confidence among participating miners, thereby reducing the perceived profitability of the attack. This strategic framework suggests a multi-layered defense mechanism aimed at maintaining the integrity and security of the LN protocol.
